risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "the sequence will be concealed until the intervention is assigned. Thus, generation of the allocation sequence and assigning of ASUs to either intervention or control group will be undertaken by the offshore independent statistician." and "randomisation within strata, this will be generated using random number generating software" Comment: The study explicitly states that allocation was generated using random number generating software by an independent statistician, which is a truly random method. Although the trial was stratified, the method used to generate the sequence is described, satisfying low-risk criteria.

Quote: "Stratification details will be provided in a de-identified form to an independent statistician located offshore and not otherwise involved in the study for randomisation within strata... the sequence will be concealed until the intervention is assigned. Thus, generation of the allocation sequence and assigning of ASUs to either intervention or control group will be undertaken by the offshore independent statistician." Comment: The protocol explicitly states that the allocation sequence was concealed until assignment and that an independent offshore statistician performed the randomisation, which is sufficient to prevent foreknowledge of upcoming group assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Following a minimum period of three months to allow the FeSS intervention to become an integral part of usual clinical practice in the intervention ASUs..." and "In those units allocated to the intervention group, the project officer (SD) also will deliver unit-based education and support." Comment: This cluster trial implemented a visible service-level intervention with workshops, education, support, and local protocols, so participants and clinical personnel delivering care could not be blinded and would know allocation. Because the outcomes include clinician behaviour and patient management measures (e.g. temperature, glucose, swallowing screening, and care compliance) that could plausibly be influenced by awareness of allocation, the dominant risk for this domain is high.

Quote: "All CATIs will be undertaken by research interviewers blind to the study design and also to ASU group allocation" and "Data entry will be undertaken by the CATI research assistants blind to group allocation"; also "Blinded outcome assessment will be undertaken for all analyses of primary and secondary outcome measures." Comment: The main patient outcomes (mRS, Barthel Index, SF-36) were assessed by blinded telephone interviewers, so outcome assessment was blinded. The outcomes are partly subjective, but because the assessors were explicitly blinded to allocation, detection bias is unlikely.

Quote: "Intention-to-treat analysis will applied [26]." and "For missing data, patient clinical data will be obtained from the TASC database. Patients themselves will already have agreed to allow access to these data as part of the study consent." Comment: The protocol describes an ITT analysis and a concrete plan to retrieve missing clinical data from the TASC database, with additional manual chart collection where needed. This suggests missing outcome data were anticipated and handled in a way that makes attrition bias unlikely; the follow-up burden at 90 days is reasonable and the paper does not signal imbalanced or outcome-related dropout.
